00:36:58 president term limits have been part of the Constitution since 00:37:05 1982 the Communist Party also proposed 00:37:11 adding Xi Jinping thought on socialism with Chinese characteristics 00:37:18 for 00:37:19 a new era to the Constitution this will make she the 3rd Chinese 00:37:26 leader to have such an honor in the past the Constitution was amended 00:37:33 to include mouthed said dong thought and done shall paying theory 00:37:39 she is expected to be elected to a 2nd 5 year term as president at 00:37:46 a meeting of the National People's Congress the proposals will likely be 00:37:52 approved at that meeting which opens on March 5th. 00:37:59 On Mario Ritter. 00:38:35 From V.O.A. 00:38:36 Learning English this is the health and lifestyle report tobacco 00:38:43 is the only legal drug that kills many of its users when used exactly as 00:38:50 intended by manufacturers that is the 1st sentence of the World 00:38:57 Health Organization's 2015 global report on smoking trends around the world 00:39:05 on its website the World Health Organization says that in 2015 over 00:39:11 1100000000 people smoked tobacco The organization also states 00:39:18 that while tobacco use is decreasing in many countries it appears to be increasing 00:39:25 in W H O eastern Mediterranean region and the African region 00:39:32 the W.H.O. 00:39:33 Lists on its website these 3 important facts about tobacco use 00:39:40 tobacco kills up to half of its users tobacco kills more than 00:39:47 7000000 people each year more than 6000000 of those deaths are 00:39:54 from direct tobacco use around 890000 nonsmokers 00:40:01 die from 2nd hand smoke. Nearly 80 percent of the world's more than 00:40:07 1000000000 smokers live in low and middle income countries 00:40:14 in 2016 the World Bank report the global state of smoking in 5 charts said that 00:40:21 from 2000 to 2015 smoking rates went up in $27.00 countries 00:40:29 the report said most of them were low and middle income countries the report 00:40:35 added that in Indonesia the rate went up by almost 30 percent it 00:40:42 said more than 70000000 people smoke in Indonesia the W.H.O. 00:40:49 Says there is 00:40:50 a tobacco crisis experts say that if we do nothing to change this trend the 00:40:57 number of people who die from tobacco each year worldwide will rise to 8000000 00:41:04 by 2030 The article also states that in every country the 00:41:11 smoking rates for men are much higher than those for women the 00:41:17 American Lung Association says fewer Americans smoke cigarettes now than before 00:41:24 tobacco control policies were established in its yearly report. 00:41:31 Says smoking rates among adults and teens are at historic lows 00:41:38 it says 00:41:39 a little more than 15.5 percent of American adults and 8 percent of 00:41:46 high school students smoke cigarettes the Centers for Disease Control and 00:41:52 Prevention in the U.S. 00:41:54 Also researches smoking rates. It says the rates in the US decreased 00:42:01 from 20.9 percent in 2005 to 15.5 percent 00:42:08 in 2016 Conny graph and or is director of the C.D.C.'s 00:42:15 office on smoking and health she says more people are quitting and those who 00:42:22 continue to smoke are smoking less However C.D.C. 00:42:27 Research also shows that almost $38000000.00 American adults still smoke 00:42:35 and the American Lung Association says tobacco use and exposure to secondhand 00:42:41 smoke affects some groups and areas of the United States more than 00:42:48 others Thomas Carr is the director of national policy for the 00:42:54 American Lung Association and writer of its 2018 report the state of 00:43:01 tobacco control Carr says smoking rates are close to 30 percent or 00:43:08 higher among poor people less educated people Native Americans and some other 00:43:15 ethnic groups Carr says that the tobacco industry advertises more to 00:43:22 some of these groups he adds that often there are more tobacco products 00:43:28 available in stores and low income areas teenagers are more 00:43:35 likely to smoke if their friends or parents smoke studies show that most people who 00:43:42 smoke start before they are 18 some start as young as age 11 00:43:49 according to the Campaign for Tobacco Free Kids Carr calls it 00:43:55 a childhood disease it starts in your teens and then once you're hooked you can't 00:44:01 get off of it. The American Lung Association advocates for the U.S. 00:44:07 Government to make buying tobacco products for those under age 21 illegal 00:44:15 the group says this would lead to 00:44:17 a drop in the number of young smokers the L.A. 00:44:22 Says if middle and high school students cannot get cigarettes they are less likely 00:44:29 to start smoking to date 5 states in the U.S. 00:44:34 California Oregon Maine Hawaii and New Jersey have raised the age to 00:44:41 21 the W.H.O. 00:44:44 Says that there are other things countries can do to limit tobacco use 00:44:50 experts at the W.H.O. 00:44:52 Say studies show that anti tobacco advertisements can reduce the number of children 00:44:59 who smoke This is especially true if the advertising uses picture warnings 00:45:06 on the packaging the W.H.O. 00:45:09 Ads that bans on tobacco advertising can reduce the use of tobacco products 00:45:17 higher taxes can also lower the number of people who use tobacco This is 00:45:23 especially true with young and poor people the C.D.C. 00:45:29 Says smokers die on average 10 years earlier than nonsmokers 00:45:37 and that's the health and lifestyle report I'm on I'm A Teo. 00:45:52 Welcome to the making of a nation American history in V.O.A. 00:45:58 Special English. South Carolina withdrew from the United States on 00:46:05 December 20th 1060 the state Issa seated because 00:46:11 a Republican Abraham Lincoln had been elected president the Republicans 00:46:18 were 00:46:19 a new party and Lincoln was the 1st to be elected president they 00:46:26 wanted to stop slavery from spreading into the western territories 00:46:33 southern states believed they had 00:46:35 a constitutional right to take property including slaves anywhere They 00:46:42 also feared that any interference with slavery would end their way of life 00:46:49 Frank Oliver and Larry West tell what happened after South Carolina left 00:46:56 the Union South Carolina faced several problems after it 00:47:03 succeeded the most serious problem was what to do with property owned 00:47:10 by the federal government there were several United States for us in and 00:47:17 around the port of Charleston for Moultrie had fewer than 70 00:47:24 soldiers Castle Pinkney had only one and Fort 00:47:30 Sumpter which was still being built had not the commander 00:47:37 of the forts asked for more men without them he said he could not 00:47:43 defend the Fort. The Army refused it told the commander 00:47:50 to defend the forts as best he could he was told to do nothing 00:47:57 that might cause South Carolina to attack if South Carolina 00:48:03 attacked or planned to attack then he could move his men into the fort 00:48:10 that would be easiest to defend that would probably be the new on 00:48:17 Fort Sumpter the governor of South Carolina planned to 00:48:24 stop any movement of federal troops he ordered state soldiers 00:48:31 to stop every boat in Charleston Harbor they were to 00:48:37 permit no United States troops to reach Fort Sumpter if 00:48:44 any boat carrying troops refused to stop the state soldiers were 00:48:51 to sink it and seized the fort 6 days 00:48:58 after South Carolina seceded from the union the commander of Charleston's 00:49:04 forts decided to move his men to Fort Sumter they would move 00:49:12 as soon as it was dark the federal troops crossed the port in 00:49:18 small boats the state soldiers did not see them the governor 00:49:25 was furious when he learned what had happened he demanded that the federal 00:49:32 troops leave Fort Sumter the commanders said they would stay 00:49:40 the governor then ordered state soldiers to cvs the other 2 forts in Charleston 00:49:46 Harbor. And he ordered the state flag raised over all other federal 00:49:53 property in the city. 00:50:04 President James Buchanan who would leave office in just 00:50:09 a few months was forced to deal with the situation his cabinet was 00:50:15 deeply divided on the issue the Southerners wanted him to recognize South 00:50:22 Carolina and order all federal troops out of Charleston Harbor 00:50:30 the northerners said he must not give up any federal property or rights 00:50:37 the president agreed to meet with 3 representatives from South Carolina 00:50:44 they had come to Washington to negotiate the future of federal property in their 00:50:50 state the attorney general said the meeting was 00:50:55 a mistake these gentlemen he said claimed to be ambassadors of 00:51:01 South Carolina this is foolish they cannot be ambassadors 00:51:09 they are lawbreakers traitors and should be arrested you cannot 00:51:15 negotiate with them the attorney general and the secretary of 00:51:22 state threatened to resign if President Buchanan gave in to South 00:51:28 Carolina's demands the president finally agreed not to give 00:51:35 in he said he would keep federal troops in Charleston Harbor 00:51:42 and he said Fort Sumpter would be defended against all hostile action 00:51:51 on the last day $1860.00. He ordered $200.00 00:51:57 troops and extra supplies sent to Fort Sumter the War 00:52:04 Department wanted to keep the operation secret so the troops and 00:52:11 supplies were put on a fast civilian ship instead of 00:52:17 a slower warship it was thought that 00:52:21 a civilian ship could get into Charleston Harbor before state forces 00:52:28 could act but Southern senator learned of the operation 00:52:35 he warned of the governor of South Carolina when the ship in 00:52:42 Charleston Harbor South Carolina soldiers were waiting the 00:52:49 soldiers lit a cannon and fired 00:52:52 a warning shot the ship refused to stop other cannons then 00:52:59 opened fire the commander of troops at Fort Sumpter had 00:53:05 a difficult decision to make he had received permission to defend the fort 00:53:12 if attacked but his orders said nothing about defending ships 00:53:19 he knew that if he opened fire the United States and South Carolina would 00:53:26 be at war the decision was made for him South 00:53:33 Carolina's cannons finally hit the ship the ship slowed 00:53:40 then turned back to see it returned north with all the 00:53:47 troops and supplies the commander of Fort Sumpter sent 00:53:53 a message to the governor of South Carolina. Your forces he wrote 00:54:01 fire this morning on 00:54:03 a civilian ship flying the flag of my government since 00:54:10 I have not been informed that South Carolina declared war on the United States 00:54:17 I can only believe that this hostile act was done without your knowledge or 00:54:24 permission for this reason and only this I did not fire 00:54:31 on your guns if the commander said the governor had 00:54:38 approved the shelling it would be an act of war and he would be forced 00:54:45 to close the Port of Charleston no ship would be permitted to enter 00:54:52 or leave the governor's answer came back within hours 00:55:00 he said South Carolina was no independent he said the attempt 00:55:07 by the United States to strengthen its force at Fort Sumpter was clearly an 00:55:13 act of aggression and he demanded that the commander surrender. 00:55:32 During the crisis over Fort Sumpter Congress tried to find 00:55:38 a compromise that might prevent war. Lawmakers proposed 00:55:44 a new line across the country south of the line slavery would be 00:55:50 permitted north of the line slavery would be illegal 00:55:58 many Republicans supported the proposal. Even though the Republican Party 00:56:04 opposed the spread of slavery into the western territories one 00:56:10 Republican however rejected the idea completely he was Abraham 00:56:17 Lincoln who would take office as president in March Lincoln said there 00:56:24 could be no compromise on extending slavery if there is he 00:56:31 said then all our hard work is lost you have trouble comes it 00:56:38 is better to let it come now than at some later time the 00:56:44 trouble would come soon one by one the states of the South 00:56:51 seceded by February 1st 00:56:56 186168 had followed South Carolina out of the 00:57:03 Union 00:57:05 a few days later representatives from the States met in Montgomery Alabama 00:57:12 their job was to create 00:57:14 a new nation it would be an independent republic called the Confederate 00:57:21 States of America the convention approved 00:57:26 a constitution for the new nation the document was like the Constitution of the 00:57:32 United States but with major changes the southern Constitution gave 00:57:39 greater importance to the rights of states and it said there could be no 00:57:46 laws against slavery the convention named former United 00:57:52 States senator Jefferson Davis to be president of the Confederate States of America 00:58:00 Davis did not want civil war but he was not afraid of it. He said 00:58:07 Our separation from the old Union is complete the time for 00:58:13 compromise has passed should others try to change our decision with 00:58:20 force they will smell Southern gun powder and feel the 00:58:26 steal of Southern swords Jefferson Davis left his farm in 00:58:33 Mississippi to become president of the Confederate States of America on February 00:58:39 11th that same day Abraham Lincoln left his home in 00:58:46 Illinois to become president of the United States as Lincoln got on the 00:58:53 train that would take him to Washington he said I now leave 00:59:00 not knowing when or whether ever I may return the task 00:59:07 before me is greater than that which rested upon our 1st president without 00:59:14 the help of God I cannot succeed without assistance I 00:59:21 cannot fail let us hope that all yet will be well. 00:59:29 And that's our program for today listen again to morrow to learn English 3 stories 00:59:36 from around the world I'm Jonathan Evans and I'm Ashley Thompson.
